texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of supercritical carbon dioxide in extraction processes emerges due to their beneficial solvent properties, non-toxicity, and environmental friendly handling properties. As an extraction medium, supercritical carbon dioxide is long known for the decaffeination of coffee [1]. Recently, supercritical carbon dioxide is tested as a drying agent for food to replace high energy demanding freeze drying processes [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In supercritical extraction processes regenerating the supercritical fluid represents the main cost constraint. Membrane technology has potential for cost efficient regeneration of water-loaded supercritical carbon dioxide. In this study we have designed membrane-based processes to dehydrate water-loaded supercritical carbon dioxide and have evaluated the processes economics as compared to those of a benchmark zeolite process. Seven flowsheet configurations have been simulated in Aspen Plus ® . In all processes a low-pressure carbon dioxide sweep stream removes water at the permeate side of the membrane, to maintain a sufficiently large driving force for water transport through the membrane. The performance of the single module has been analyzed to determine suitable process variables, such as the magnitude of sweep gas flow. We identify three flowsheet configurations each having individual benefits: Configuration 1 is most simple but highly sensitive towards changing process parameters, configuration 2 is CO2-emission-free, and configuration 3 is most insensitive towards membrane selectivity. With configuration 3 a cost reduction of 20% compared to the benchmark zeolite adsorption is identified: also the process is continuous and free of cycling of gas streams.
texts.text: In supercritical extraction processes regenerating the supercritical fluid represents the main cost constraint. Membrane technology has potential for cost efficient regeneration of water-loaded supercritical carbon dioxide. In this study we have designed membrane-based processes to dehydrate water-loaded supercritical carbon dioxide and have evaluated the processes economics as compared to those of a benchmark zeolite process. Seven flowsheet configurations have been simulated in Aspen Plus ® . In all processes a low-pressure carbon dioxide sweep stream removes water at the permeate side of the membrane, to maintain a sufficiently large driving force for water transport through the membrane. The performance of the single module has been analyzed to determine suitable process variables, such as the magnitude of sweep gas flow. We identify three flowsheet configurations each having individual benefits: Configuration 1 is most simple but highly sensitive towards changing process parameters, configuration 2 is CO2-emission-free, and configuration 3 is most insensitive towards membrane selectivity. With configuration 3 a cost reduction of 20% compared to the benchmark zeolite adsorption is identified: also the process is continuous and free of cycling of gas streams.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Birtigh and Brunner [6] studied different regeneration methods for loaded supercritical fluids. Several publications and patents are available on the regeneration of supercritical fluids with porous and microporous membranes such as for the separation of caffeine from supercritical carbon dioxide [7-9]. Yet limited publications have been reported on the subject of removing water from supercritical carbon dioxide with dense polymeric membranes [10]. Such dense membranes are known to accomplish the separation of sub-critical carbon dioxide and water [11,12]. For low or ambient pressure systems numerous papers and patents are available on the drying and dehumidification of air and other gases with membranes especially on the field of air conditioning systems using sweep streams. Among them are several patents with drying methods that propose flowsheet designs similar to the investigated flowsheet configurations in this study [12-15]. Little is known on process configuration for drying processes at supercritical gas conditions.

texts.orig: This study investigates possible process configurations in which highly selective membranes regenerate loaded supercritical carbon dioxide. We examine whether membrane technology is a suitable alternative to adsorption on zeolites. Process economics as well as particular process characteristics are critically evaluated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We extended a model for a hollow fiber membrane module developed by Scholz et al. [16]. In this model the permeation through the membrane is described by the solution-diffusion model [17]. The 3-End model developed by Scholz et al. [16] was expanded to a 4-End module to allow for the use of a sweep stream. Changing the boundary conditions links the sweep's temperature T , pressure p , enthalpy h , molar composition x and molar volume v to the model variables.

texts.orig: The model accounts for the following non-ideal effects: (i) real fluid behavior and (ii) the temperature change according to Joule-Thomson effect at depressurization of the gas crossing the membrane. Since the behavior of a supercritical fluid close to saturation with water is highly non-ideal, properly describing real gas fluid behavior is crucial.

texts.orig: Fig. 2 illustrates the relation between D and the required membrane surface area. The non-linear increase in the required membrane surface area is in particular steep for D exceeding 95% at the implemented conditions. This graph depends on the applied process variables but the main characteristic pertains. In the following, we set the required value for D to 95% for two reasons. Firstly, for higher D the sharp increase in required membrane surface area might not be justified by the moderate benefit in the process performance. Secondly, high values of D also require highly dried sweep streams.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several gases can be used as sweep gas. The chosen sweep gas will inevitably be present in the retentate stream because, if not present in the feed, it will permeate through the membrane against the pressure gradient until partial pressure equilibrium. Since small amounts of sweep substance enter the food drying chamber, the sweep substance should comply with the following characteristics: (i) non-toxic, (ii) odorless, (iii) inflammable, and (iv) inert. We chose CO2 because it is cheap and already present in the process. As a feed substance CO2 allows to recycle a sweep stream and mix it with feed or retentate stream. Air might also be interesting if the amount of reactive oxygen can be tolerated.

Few of the available equation of states in Aspen Plus ® are able to determine physical properties close to the critical point realistically. However, appropriate choice of the method is crucial for simulation results. Fig. 3 shows isothermal saturation curves simulated with two different models next to measured data from different literature sources. Below, the processes have been simulated with Predictive Soave Redlich Kwong (PSRK). For comparison all processes were also simulated with the second-best method Schwarzentuber-Renon (SR-Polar). This comparison quantifies the sensitivity of the simulation to different property models. Table 1 summarizes the fixed process parameters.

texts.orig: Table 2 lists the economics of the zeolite process. Investment costs comprise costs for two vessels and a heater. The operational expenses include costs for the zeolite itself and the regeneration of the loaded zeolite with hot air and are calculated based on a shortcut model presented in Bathen [38]. For the calculation we assume a zeolite of type 3A, a cycle period of 12 h [39,37], a regeneration energy of 3.3 kW h / kg H 2 O [39] and a regeneration temperature of 260 ◦ C [32]. With operational costs of 3200Euro/a the zeolite regeneration costs amount to 20 ct kg zeolite per required amount of zeolite.

texts.orig: Three process variables influence the separation performance considerably: the magnitude of sweep stream, the permeate pressure, and the water content in the sweep. The values of these variables are chosen individually for each flowsheet to obtain a good process behavior in each configuration.
texts.text: Three process variables influence the separation performance considerably: the magnitude of sweep stream, the permeate pressure, and the water content in the sweep. The values of these variables are chosen individually for each flowsheet to obtain a good process behavior in each configuration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Optimal process design requires implementation of the individual flowsheet configurations in a tool that optimizes the process variables, the dehydration degree, minimal energy consumption, and minimal costs. Since in this study no structural optimization is performed, a good knowledge on the module performance is required to choose the mentioned variables reasonably. This section investigates the influence of these three variables on the depletion performance in the single module. Table 4 presents the resulting ranges for the individual variables.

texts.orig: Recycled sweep gas contains residual water. The water content in the sweep stream influences the transmembrane water flux by two effects. First, the amount of permeating water depends on the saturation of the sweep gas. A sweep gas with already increased water content can dissolve less permeated water than a dry sweep stream. However, larger sweep streams compensate this effect easily.
texts.text: Recycled sweep gas contains residual water. The water content in the sweep stream influences the transmembrane water flux by two effects. First, the amount of permeating water depends on the saturation of the sweep gas. A sweep gas with already increased water content can dissolve less permeated water than a dry sweep stream. However, larger sweep streams compensate this effect easily.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Second, the water content in the sweep inlet determines the maximal reachable dehydration degree in the retentate. At too high concentrations the partial pressure difference of water tends too zero before the required dehydration degree of 95% is reached. This effect cannot be compensated with larger sweep streams but with the sweep not exceeding a maximum water content. The maximal water content in the sweep inlet can simply be estimated from the driving force in the solution-diffusion-model. Depleting the feed always requires the partial pressure of the feed to be greater than the partial pressure in the permeate, thus:

texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the most simple design to perform the dehydration, a single stage membrane setup. Water vapor permeates through the membrane until the supercritical CO2 is depleted to 95%. The sweep gas collects the water on the permeate side. Since also CO2 permeates through the membrane, a make-up stream of dry CO2 is required to keep the mass of product CO2 constant. The make-up CO2 is compressed to 100 bar and fed to the product gas stream. Aspen Plus ® calculates the required membrane area to meet the final depletion degree. The magnitude of the membrane area is rather low, due the small feed stream and large permeabilities.

texts.orig: The main advantage of the process are the limited demands with respect to process equipment. Additionally, the sweep stream is comparably small. The small sweep is due to the atmospheric pressure at the permeate side, implying a higher saturation capacity of CO2. On the other hand the process demands a continuous fresh CO2 supply for sweep and make-up CO2, and a high energy demand for compressing the large make-up CO2 stream. Even though the membrane has a high H2O/CO2 selectivity, the permeated CO2 accounts for approximately 1/3 of the released permeate stream because the mole fraction of CO2 in the feed is high.

texts.orig: Prior to the condenser the stream is compressed to 55 bar. According to Fig. 3, the saturation curve of water in CO2 shows a minimal water content at this pressure. Here, less deep condensation temperatures are necessary (25 ◦ C) to perform a sufficient sweep drying. Part of the dried permeate is re-used as sweep gas. To increase the CO2 yield the other part of the compressed permeate is further compressed and recycled to the feed stream. To facilitate the compression and to limit the required compression stages to three, the sweep pressure is set to 2.5 bar.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flowsheet 3 contains two membrane stages, one to perform the dehydration of the feed and a second to purify the sweep stream. Fig. 9 illustrates the flowsheet design. Flowsheet 3 also contains a compression of the sweep stream to 55 bar with a subsequent condensation to remove H2O from the process. Main idea in this flowsheet is to dry excess CO2 to a high drying degree and to mix it with the product stream. Since solely condensation cannot reach this drying degree, a second membrane stage performs the drying. The process additionally benefits from a highly dried sweep stream. With the high transmembrane pressure in the second membrane and the small size of the stream little membrane areas are required to purify the stream and thus little CO2 passes the membrane. Therefore, this flowsheet is not CO2 emission-free, but the emitted stream is small compared to the CO2 emissions in flowsheet 1. To compensate for CO2 loss make-up CO2 is added to the process as sweep gas to the first membrane stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Air can be used as sweep gas whenever it is not mixed with a product stream and small amounts of it are allowed to enter the extraction cycle. Two effects are studied in case air replaces CO2 as sweep gas, namely the reduction of total CO2 emissions and the economic effect of less operating costs for CO2 expenses. Because air is applied where the sweep stream is emitted to the atmosphere, the reduction of CO2 emissions can be substantial.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5.1. Selectivity of the membrane
texts.text: 3.5.1. Selectivity of the membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The selectivity of the membrane mainly determines the transmembrane CO2 flux at constant depletion degrees. This greatly influences the process economics since the compression and makeup CO2 costs contribute foremost to the operational costs. A membrane swelling due to water and CO2 sorption can cause a great loss of selectivity especially at elevated temperatures [41]. On the other hand further development and cross linking of the membrane material might stabilize selectivities [42]. Investigating the stability towards changing selectivities is especially interesting concerning long term plasticization and selectivity reduction.

Because it is independent from the price of CO2, it becomes less expensive than flowsheet 3 at increasing CO2 costs. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flowsheet 3 performs least expensive for the base case with a cost reduction of 20% compared to zeolite. Since the total annual costs of this option are mainly dominated by investment costs for compressors, the economy of scale effect would promote this flowsheet at a scale-up to higher feed flow rates. Main advantage of the process is a high stability towards decreasing selectivities making this option advantageous as a plasticization resistant process for long time operation of the process. Additionally, CO2 emissions reduce to only 0.05% of the feed flow rate for an operation with air as the sweep gas in the second membrane stage. Also the depletion degree could be increased to 99% by a cost increase of about 12% and still be favorable compared with the benchmark process.

Since the total annual costs of this option are mainly dominated by investment costs for compressors, the economy of scale effect would promote this flowsheet at a scale-up to higher feed flow rates. Main advantage of the process is a high stability towards decreasing selectivities making this option advantageous as a plasticization resistant process for long time operation of the process. Additionally, CO2 emissions reduce to only 0.05% of the feed flow rate for an operation with air as the sweep gas in the second membrane stage. Also the depletion degree could be increased to 99% by a cost increase of about 12% and still be favorable compared with the benchmark process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To conclude, for long time operation and a scale-up to larger plants, flowsheet 3 is the most promising. It profits from a safe operation with high stability towards changing selectivities and emits low amounts of CO2. However, for changing conditions or membrane parameters, flowsheet 1 and 2 should be taken into consideration. Especially for higher selectivities of the membrane, flowsheet 1 is the favored option.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Seven different membrane processes for the drying of supercritical carbon dioxide have been developed and simulated. We investigated the flowsheet configurations based on an technoeconomic evaluation and a sensitivity analysis.
texts.text: Seven different membrane processes for the drying of supercritical carbon dioxide have been developed and simulated. We investigated the flowsheet configurations based on an technoeconomic evaluation and a sensitivity analysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three of the designs, flowsheet 1, 2 and 3, have been identified to be promising process configurations that can economically compete with the benchmark zeolite process. The main assets of the flowsheets are: Flowsheet 1 has a high potential for costs reduction when air is applied as sweep gas or in case of high selectivities. Flowsheet 2 features no CO2 emissions and can be considered green. Flowsheet 3 amounts to the lowest cost in the base case. It achieves a cost reduction of 20% compared with the benchmark. Its main advantage is a remarkable stability towards decreasing selectivities that could occur if the membrane tends to plasticize.

Flowsheet 2 features no CO2 emissions and can be considered green. Flowsheet 3 amounts to the lowest cost in the base case. It achieves a cost reduction of 20% compared with the benchmark. Its main advantage is a remarkable stability towards decreasing selectivities that could occur if the membrane tends to plasticize.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figs. A.14-A.17 show flowsheet configurations that have been investigated and identified to be less efficient than flowsheet 1, 2 and 3. However, the configurations might become interesting for changing conditions. The setups are presented shortly.
texts.text: Figs. A.14-A.17 show flowsheet configurations that have been investigated and identified to be less efficient than flowsheet 1, 2 and 3. However, the configurations might become interesting for changing conditions. The setups are presented shortly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flowsheet 4, illustrated in Fig. A.14, includes a condensation step to regenerate the permeate stream and recycle it as sweep gas. In this process configuration condensation and sweep operate at the same pressure (2 bar). This pressure can be chosen freely, though condensation pressure and sweep pressure compose a trade-off. Low pressures provide high saturation capacities for H2O in the sweep and keep the sweep size low, while high pressures facilitate condensation of H2O. The condensation temperature should to be higher than 0 ◦ C to prevent freezing within the condenser. The minimal condensation temperature is set to 3 ◦ C. In order to condense enough water from the sweep gas to perform the required depletion degree in the retentate of 95% the condensation pressure is set to 2 bar. However, a disadvantage of the process is that still at 2 bar a sufficient drying of the sweep cannot be achieved. The results show, that the required mass of refill CO2 in flowsheet 4 is higher than sweep and refill in flowsheet 1. Hence, it can be expected, that this process will not be economically beneficial. The process performance could probably be improved by adding a compression before the condensation and a subsequent turbine to run the membrane process and the condensation unit at different pressures. However, the compressor and the turbine require additional investment costs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flowsheet 5 contains two membrane stages, one to perform the dehydration of the feed and a second to purify the sweep stream. Fig. A.15 illustrates the flowsheet design. Configuration 5 contains a compression of the sweep stream to 55 bar with a subsequent condensation to remove H2O from the process according to flowsheet 2. The main idea in this flowsheet is to dry excess CO2 to a high drying degree and to mix it with the product stream. A second membrane stage perform the drying to the very high drying degree. With the high transmembrane pressure in the second membrane and the small size of the stream little membrane areas are required to purify the stream and thus little CO2 crosses the membrane. Thus, the emitted stream is small. The required make-up CO2 to compensate the loss in the second membrane stage is fed as sweep gas to the first membrane stage. This setup shows to be less efficient than flowsheet 3 due to an elevated water content in the recycled sweep.

With the high transmembrane pressure in the second membrane and the small size of the stream little membrane areas are required to purify the stream and thus little CO2 crosses the membrane. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flowsheet 6 is similar to flowsheet 3 with the only difference in recycling the permeate of the second membrane stage. In this setup an elevated permeate pressure of 2.5 bar in the second membrane stage is used. In order to preserve the permeated CO2 in the second membrane stage, the stream is partially recycled to the condensation step with the upstream compression and mixed with the sweep of the first module. A purge stream is necessary to remove the added CO2 of the second sweep and keep the total amount of CO2 in the product constant. However, in this flowsheet the loss in the purge stream is larger than the loss of permeated stream in flowsheet 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Flowsheet 7 comprises two membranes, which are, contrarily to the previous flowsheets, connected in series. Fig. A.17 depicts this process configuration. Main idea of this option is to split the separation into two parts, first a bulk separation with high water concentrations in the permeate and second a polishing step to finally reach to desired depletion degree. The dry sweep enters the second membrane stage and solves the residual water. After this membrane stage the stream splits in two parts, one decompressed to 1 bar and serving as a sweep stream to the first stage, while the other is compressed in a multistage compressor with interstage cooling to remove water from the process and recycled to the feed. The split fraction of the sweep is adjusted by Aspen Plus ® , so that the dried product stream has the same size as the inserted CO2 with the feed. The first membrane only needs a little sweep stream because H2O is not removed completely to the final depletion degree. In addition, it can be operated at ambient permeate pressure because the saturated stream is emitted.
